title: Sideshow Bob Roberts

jokes.text: That Barlow's a right-wing crackpot. He said Ted Kennedy lacked integrity. Can you believe that?
jokes.setup: Homer calls Barlow a crackpot for making political accusations
jokes.punchline: The accusation about Ted Kennedy actually being reasonable makes Homer's outrage absurd
jokes.explanation: Classic Homer political obliviousness - defending Ted Kennedy's integrity without irony shows perfect character-specific stupidity

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You're a crackpot, Barlow! Making all these wild political accusations!

dialogue.speaker: Barlow
dialogue.line: Even a broken clock is right twice a day, Simpson. Ted Kennedy was actually involved in some questionable dealings.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's... wait, that one's actually true? Well, I'm still mad about the other stuff!

jokes.text: I consider myself politically correct. His views make me uncomfortable.
jokes.setup: Marge positions herself as progressive and sensitive
jokes.punchline: Uses 'politically correct' incorrectly, showing she doesn't understand the term she's invoking
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Marge's well-intentioned but confused understanding of political terminology

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: I'm very progressive and sensitive to others' feelings.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: That's why I always make sure to be politically correct... by never saying anything that might offend me.

jokes.text: I usually think people who vote are a bit fruity. But for some reason, this Birch Barlow really speaks to me.
jokes.setup: Homer dismisses all voters as crazy
jokes.punchline: Immediately contradicts himself by being swayed by the radio host
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic - anti-political stance immediately abandoned when he finds someone he likes. Great character consistency.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: All voters are crazy. Nobody in their right mind votes.

dialogue.speaker: Radio Host
dialogue.line: Vote for candidate X!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That makes sense! I'm voting for candidate X!

jokes.text: our six-term mayor... the illiterate, tax-cheating, pot-smoking spend-o-crat... Diamond Joe Quimby. Hey, I am no longer illiterate.
jokes.setup: Barlow lists Quimby's many character flaws including illiteracy
jokes.punchline: Quimby only objects to the illiteracy charge, implicitly accepting all other accusations
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character moment - Quimby's selective denial reveals both his priorities and confirms the other accusations through omission

dialogue.speaker: Birch Barlow
dialogue.line: You're a tax cheat, a draft dodger, a wife beater!

dialogue.speaker: Mayor Quimby
dialogue.line: Now, now, I think the charges are a bit inflated.

dialogue.speaker: Birch Barlow
dialogue.line: And illiterate!

dialogue.speaker: Mayor Quimby
dialogue.line: Hey, I resent that! I went to Brown!

jokes.text: Well, I'm doing a school project on fireworks.
jokes.setup: Bart claims to have a legitimate school project
jokes.punchline: The 'project' is obviously just an excuse to play with fireworks
jokes.explanation: Standard Bart mischief setup - functional but predictable

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Mom, Dad, I need you to sign this permission slip for my school project.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: School project? What kind of project?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: It's about the, uh, chemical properties of explosives. Very educational.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Bart, what is that?

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: What? Oh, this? Just some bottles and fuses for the, uh, science part.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Fireworks! Woo-hoo!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad! It's for school!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Right, right. School fireworks. The best kind.

jokes.text: Bart, I wish you wouldn't lie like that.
jokes.setup: Bart's obvious lie about a fireworks project
jokes.punchline: Marge's understated disappointment rather than anger
jokes.explanation: Classic Marge reaction - gentle but knowing, perfectly in character

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Bart, that's the worst lie I've ever heard. And I'm married to a man who once told me he was working late at the nuclear plant when he was actually at a donut shop.

jokes.text: What a week. He promise a big firework display. Bad student. Bad principal.
jokes.setup: Visiting Chinese principal commenting on Bart's promised fireworks
jokes.punchline: Broken English delivery and mutual blame assessment
jokes.explanation: Dated stereotype humor that relies on accent comedy - functional but not sophisticated

dialogue.speaker: Chinese Principal
dialogue.line: You promise fireworks, but I see only disappointment. In China, we have saying: 'Man who promise much and deliver little is like firecracker that go 'poof' instead of 'BOOM!'

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dude, that's not my fault! The fireworks were supposed to be epic!

dialogue.speaker: Chinese Principal
dialogue.line: Supposed to be? In my country, we not say 'supposed to be.' We say 'is' or 'is not.' Your American way very confusing. Very disappointing.

jokes.text: When I'm driving the car, I get to choose the radio station. When you're driving, we'll listen to your radio station.
jokes.setup: Standard parental logic about car privileges
jokes.punchline: The obvious fact that Marge will never drive/choose
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Homer's false reasonableness masking his control

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You can't drive the car until you're 16.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: But Dad, I'm already 10!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, you've got 6 more years to wait.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: What about Mom? Can she drive?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Your mother will never drive this car. She chose me.

jokes.text: It's time people realized we conservatives aren't all Johnny Hatemongers... and Charlie Bible-Thumps or even, God forbid, George Bushes.
jokes.setup: Bob defending conservatives against stereotypes
jokes.punchline: Made-up names that perfectly capture the stereotypes, plus Bush as worst case
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay creating perfectly named strawmen, with George Bush as the ultimate insult. Shows Bob's intellect and political awareness.

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: I'm not a racist, but conservatives aren't all bad. We have diversity of thought. There's Rush Limbaugh, there's Ann Coulter, there's Sean Hannity—okay, bad examples. But what about... uh... there's... well, there's George W. Bush. And he's the worst.

jokes.text: I'll spare you the embarrassment of admitting you don't know who Bob is.
jokes.setup: Lisa being condescending to her family
jokes.punchline: Her assumption that they're too ignorant to recognize an obvious voice
jokes.explanation: Good Lisa character moment - her intellectual superiority complex on display

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Of course you wouldn't recognize that voice. It's from a obscure 1987 French New Wave film that only people with a modicum of intellectual sophistication would appreciate.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Lisa, that's Krusty the Clown.

jokes.text: I'm only 10, and I already got two mortal enemies.
jokes.setup: Bart processing that Sideshow Bob is his enemy
jokes.punchline: The absurd reality of a 10-year-old having multiple mortal enemies
jokes.explanation: Great meta-joke about the show's premise - a child having arch-nemeses is genuinely absurd when stated plainly

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Wait a minute. Sideshow Bob is my enemy? My mortal enemy? I'm ten years old! How many mortal enemies does a kid need?

jokes.text: Sleep. Sexy dames, and plenty of them. Matlock!
jokes.setup: Politicians asking elderly constituents what they want
jokes.punchline: Escalating from reasonable to inappropriate to obsessive TV show reference
jokes.explanation: Perfect elderly stereotype subversion - starts predictable then gets wonderfully specific with Matlock obsession

dialogue.speaker: Politician
dialogue.line: What do you elderly residents want from your government?

dialogue.speaker: Elderly Resident 1
dialogue.line: Better healthcare and Social Security.

dialogue.speaker: Elderly Resident 2
dialogue.line: Cheaper prescription drugs.

dialogue.speaker: Elderly Resident 3
dialogue.line: Free shuffleboard!

dialogue.speaker: Elderly Resident 4
dialogue.line: A government-mandated nap time at 2 PM.

dialogue.speaker: Elderly Resident 5
dialogue.line: Soft foods only. No more chewing.

dialogue.speaker: Elderly Resident 1
dialogue.line: We want you to cancel all TV shows except Matlock!

dialogue.speaker: Elderly Resident 2
dialogue.line: And if any show comes on before Matlock, we riot!

jokes.text: Attempted murder. Now, honestly, what is that? Do they give a Nobel Prize for attempted chemistry? Do they?
jokes.setup: Bob defending himself against attempted murder charges
jokes.punchline: Absurd parallel comparing attempted murder to attempted chemistry
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist logic that perfectly captures Bob's intellectual arrogance. The chemistry parallel is completely illogical but delivered with such confidence it becomes hilarious. Highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: Your Honor, I move that we set aside the charges of attempted murder. After all, what is attempted murder but failed murder? And if we punish failed attempts, then every unsuccessful chemistry student should be in jail!

jokes.text: Just like Colonel Oliver North, Officer Stacey Koon... and cartoon smokesperson Joe Camel.
jokes.setup: Barlow listing conservative martyrs
jokes.punchline: Escalating from real controversial figures to a cartoon cigarette mascot
jokes.explanation: Great satirical escalation showing how conservative talk radio equates serious political figures with advertising mascots

dialogue.speaker: Birch Barlow
dialogue.line: They're coming after me next! Just like they came after Rush Limbaugh, just like they came after Oliver North, just like they came after that guy from McLintock!

dialogue.speaker: Birch Barlow
dialogue.line: And now they're after Joe Camel!

jokes.text: All right, you heard the man. One grenade each. Oh, Jeez. Really? You think so? All right, give them back. Come on. Everybody, give them back. Hey. Hey! Who pulled the pin on this one?
jokes.setup: Moe misinterprets call for political action as call for violence
jokes.punchline: Escalation to actual grenade danger with typical Moe incompetence
jokes.explanation: Perfect Moe character moment - violent misunderstanding followed by dangerous incompetence. The pulled pin escalates it perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Caller
dialogue.line: We need to throw grenades at City Hall!

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: Finally! A call to action I can get behind!

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: Alright fellas, let's go throw some grenades!

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: Wait, what?!

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: I got 'em right here. Nice and fresh from my basement.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Moe, those are real grenades!

dialogue.speaker: Moe
dialogue.line: D'oh! I thought they were metaphorical!

jokes.text: Very well, if that is the way the winds are blowing... let no one say I don't also blow.
jokes.setup: Quimby trying to sound statesmanlike about political pressure
jokes.punchline: Unintentional sexual innuendo with 'blow'
jokes.explanation: Classic unintentional innuendo that fits Quimby's pompous but clueless character

dialogue.speaker: Mayor Quimby
dialogue.line: My fellow citizens, I assure you that no amount of pressure will make me blow this election.

jokes.text: Congratulations, Robert Underdunk Terwilliger.
jokes.setup: Formal pardoning ceremony
jokes.punchline: Sideshow Bob's ridiculous middle name revealed
jokes.explanation: Good character detail joke - the absurd middle name contrasts with Bob's pretentious persona

dialogue.speaker: Mayor Quimby
dialogue.line: I hereby pardon Sideshow Bob Underpants Terwilliger!

jokes.text: A fine mahoq to you all.
jokes.setup: Bob greeting his political backers
jokes.punchline: Made-up greeting that sounds pretentious and foreign
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Bob's pretentious attempts at sophistication through made-up formal language

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: Buongiorno, my fellow Republicans. Or as I like to say... Bonjour, mes amis capitalistes!

jokes.text: There is to be no wising off... no face-making and no grass-eating. This means you, Ralph.
jokes.setup: Standard behavioral expectations for students
jokes.punchline: Escalating to absurd 'grass-eating' specifically targeted at Ralph
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ralph joke - the idea that he specifically needs to be told not to eat grass is both absurd and believable for his character

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: Now, I want all of you students to behave yourselves and follow the rules.

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: That means no running in the halls, no talking back to teachers, and absolutely no eating the grass on the school lawn.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph Wiggum
dialogue.line: But Principal Skinner, I like eating the grass.

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: Well, Wiggum, starting today, grass-eating is strictly prohibited. No grass-eating before school, during school, or after school.

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: No grass-eating on weekends, holidays, or during summer vacation.

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: And Wiggum, no eating the grass in the cafeteria, the gymnasium, or the parking lot either!

jokes.text: Do you know what he does? He flip-flops. Sometimes he doesn't know whether he's coming or going.
jokes.setup: Bob criticizing Quimby's inconsistency
jokes.punchline: Visual of Bob literally demonstrating flip-flopping and confusion with exaggerated gestures
jokes.explanation: Standard political criticism made visual through Bob's theatrical delivery, functional but not exceptional

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: Mayor Quimby's inconsistency is truly remarkable. One day he supports the arts, the next he's cutting funding entirely!

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: The man flip-flops more than a fish out of water!

jokes.text: Help! I'm being attacked by things.
jokes.setup: Bart needs to create a distraction from Bob's speech
jokes.punchline: Deliberately vague and dramatic cry for help
jokes.explanation: Good Bart mischief moment - the vagueness of 'things' makes it both suspicious and funny

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Help! Help! I'm being repressed!

jokes.text: No children have ever meddled with the Republican Party and lived to tell about it
jokes.setup: Bob threatening children who interfered
jokes.punchline: Treating political opposition as literally life-threatening
jokes.explanation: Good dark comedy showing Bob's villainous nature through over-the-top political threat

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: You meddling children have made a grave mistake. I will crush my political enemies with the same ruthlessness I've crushed you!

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: In fact, I don't see any difference between a political opponent and a mortal threat. They're all equally deserving of my wrath!

jokes.text: Stay out of Riverdale.
jokes.setup: Threatening message to the Simpson children
jokes.punchline: Reference to Archie Comics universe as threatening location
jokes.explanation: Clever meta-joke that treats wholesome Archie Comics as sinister threat. Good economy and originality.

dialogue.speaker: Mr. Burns' goon
dialogue.line: We know where you live. We know where you go to school. And if you don't pay up, we're gonna send you to... Riverdale.

jokes.text: This time, he's the lesser of two evils. Yeah, yeah, I love Grimby.
jokes.setup: Campaign worker's honest but terrible slogan
jokes.punchline: Barney's drunken mishearing of Quimby's name
jokes.explanation: Good character moments - terrible campaign slogan paired with typical Barney confusion

dialogue.speaker: Quimby campaign worker
dialogue.line: Our new campaign slogan is 'Quimby: He's Adequate.'

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: Quimby? I thought you said 'Gimme! He's a duck!'

jokes.text: Not many people know I owned the first radio in Springfield. Weren't much on the air then. Just Edison reciting the alphabet over and over. 'A,' he'd say. Then 'B.' 'C' would usually follow.
jokes.setup: Old man sharing historical anecdote about early radio
jokes.punchline: Absurdly boring early radio content described in excruciating detail
jokes.explanation: Brilliant absurdist humor - the idea of Edison just reciting the alphabet, described with painful mundane detail, perfectly captures boring old stories

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Back in my day, we didn't have your fancy television. We had radio! And let me tell you, it was just as boring. There was a fellow named Herbert who would read the phone book for three hours every evening. A-B-C, Anderson, Andrew, 555-1234. Then he'd move on to the D's. We'd sit there, the whole family, just listening. Sometimes my wife would fall asleep, and I'd have to nudge her awake so she wouldn't miss the exciting transition from the D's to the E's. And the sponsors! Oh boy, the sponsors would interrupt every ten minutes to tell us about their new laxative. 'Golden Goose Laxative - it'll clear you out faster than you can say Eisenhower!' We thought we were living in the lap of luxury.

jokes.text: Even though we're being broadcast on Fox... there's no need for obnoxious hooting and hollering.
jokes.setup: Moderator addressing debate audience
jokes.punchline: Self-aware joke about Fox network's reputation
jokes.explanation: Good meta-humor that allows Simpsons to comment on its own network's audience reputation

dialogue.speaker: Larry King
dialogue.line: Welcome to tonight's debate. I'm your moderator, Larry King, broadcasting live on Fox — the network that brought you 'Cops,' 'America's Most Wanted,' and apparently, now, the news.

jokes.text: I'd say that Les Wynan ought to do more thinking and less whining. There's no Councilman Les Wynan. Good line, though.
jokes.setup: Bob delivers clever political putdown
jokes.punchline: King reveals Bob just made up the person he's insulting
jokes.explanation: Excellent joke showing Bob's quick wit creating fictional targets for his clever wordplay. King's acknowledgment makes it even funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: Your political commentary is as substantive as a cardboard cutout of a senator!

dialogue.speaker: Larry King
dialogue.line: Bob, Senator Fitzwilliam doesn't exist.

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: Well, that explains why he never returned my calls.

jokes.text: But suppose for a second that your house was ransacked by thugs... your family tied up in the basement with socks in their mouths. You try to open the door, but there's too much blood on the knob. What is your question? My question's about the budget.
jokes.setup: Extremely violent and specific crime scenario building dramatic tension
jokes.punchline: Anticlimactic shift to mundane budget question
jokes.explanation: Masterful misdirection - builds incredibly dark, specific scenario then completely abandons it for boring budget talk. Perfect structure and timing.

dialogue.speaker: Larry King
dialogue.line: I'm going to describe an extremely violent and specific crime scenario that builds dramatic tension, and then ask you a mundane budget question.

jokes.text: I don't agree with his Bart-killing policy... but I do approve of his Selma-killing policy.
jokes.setup: Homer weighing political pros and cons
jokes.punchline: Casual discussion of murder policies toward family members
jokes.explanation: Dark humor that perfectly captures Homer's selfishness and family dynamics. The casual treatment of murder policies is brilliantly absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Let's see... pro: I get to keep my money. Con: I have to kill my family.

jokes.text: Well, he framed me for armed robbery... but, man, I'm aching for that upper-class tax cut.
jokes.setup: Krusty acknowledging Bob's crimes against him
jokes.punchline: Self-interest overriding personal grievances for tax benefits
jokes.explanation: Great political satire showing how economic self-interest trumps personal grievances. Perfect Krusty character moment.

dialogue.speaker: Krusty
dialogue.line: Yeah, Bob, you've done some terrible things to me over the years. But I'm willing to let bygones be bygones if there's a tax write-off in it for me.

jokes.text: For Joe Quimby, 1 percent. And we remind you, there is a 1-percent margin of error.
jokes.setup: Election results showing Bob's landslide victory
jokes.punchline: Quimby's vote total exactly matching the margin of error
jokes.explanation: Clever statistical joke implying Quimby may have gotten zero actual votes. Good economy and mathematical humor.

dialogue.speaker: News Anchor
dialogue.line: We're calling the race for Bob. He wins by a landslide.

dialogue.speaker: News Anchor
dialogue.line: Mayor Quimby's vote total is within the margin of error.

jokes.text: It's the rapture! Quick, get Bart out of the house before God comes.
jokes.setup: Springfield residents celebrating Bob's victory with fireworks
jokes.punchline: Homer's assumption it's the apocalypse and desire to hide Bart from God
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character moment showing Homer's awareness that Bart would definitely not make it to heaven. Dark religious humor perfectly executed.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: It's the end of the world! We're all gonna die!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, it's just fireworks.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Fireworks? That's what they want us to think! Bart, get in the basement! We need to hide you from God!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, chill out.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: No time for chilling! God's got a list, and you're probably on it!

jokes.text: At that time, we will blow up your house and any remaining Simpsons
jokes.setup: Bob giving eviction notice for expressway construction
jokes.punchline: His casual mention of killing family members as part of construction project
jokes.explanation: Good dark humor showing Bob's villainous nature disguised as municipal policy

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: I'm afraid I have some bad news. The state has decided to build a new expressway, and your home is right in the path.

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: An expressway? But where will we go?

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: Well, I'm sure they'll relocate you somewhere. Of course, there may be a few... complications during construction. Casualties, if you will.

jokes.text: Move your goddurn house, son.
jokes.setup: Family discussing how to stop the expressway
jokes.punchline: Grampa's unhelpful literalism and old-fashioned cursing
jokes.explanation: Standard Grampa moment - unhelpful advice delivered with conviction. Functional character humor.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: We gotta stop that expressway! I'll chain myself to the bulldozer!

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: Blast it all! This is worse than the time they paved over my favorite spitting rock!

dialogue.speaker: Grampa Simpson
dialogue.line: In my day, we didn't have expressways. We had oxcarts, and we liked it!

jokes.text: Well, yes. But not for four or five years. Bart, you're going to kindergarten.
jokes.setup: Bart learning he has to repeat fourth grade
jokes.punchline: Even worse punishment of going back to kindergarten
jokes.explanation: Good escalation joke - punishment gets progressively worse in absurd way

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: I'm afraid you'll have to repeat fourth grade, Bart.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: What?! That's not fair!

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: Actually, we're considering an even worse punishment.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: What could be worse than that?

dialogue.speaker: Principal Skinner
dialogue.line: Kindergarten.

jokes.text: I like talking to you.
jokes.setup: Bart using kindergarten toy phone
jokes.punchline: The toy phone apparently talking back to him
jokes.explanation: Mild absurdist moment playing on toy phone trope. Functional but not exceptional.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Hello? Hello?

dialogue.speaker: Toy Phone
dialogue.line: Hi Bart!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Whoa! This thing actually works!

jokes.text: I can't believe a convicted felon would get so many votes... and another convicted felon would get so few.
jokes.setup: Lisa analyzing the suspicious election results
jokes.punchline: Noting both candidates are felons, making the disparity suspicious
jokes.explanation: Smart observation that points out the absurdity of Springfield's mayoral choices while advancing plot

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Both candidates are felons? That makes the disparity even more suspicious!

jokes.text: Except their dad wasn't waiting in the car reading Archie Comics. Stuck-up Riverdale punks. Think they're too good for me.
jokes.setup: Lisa comparing them to Woodward and Bernstein
jokes.punchline: Homer's insecurity about reading comics plus callback to earlier Riverdale threat
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Homer's defensiveness and nice callback to earlier Archie Comics reference

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Comics? I thought you said COMICS! I can't read those things, there's too many words!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Dad, they're investigating the mayor. Like Woodward and Bernstein.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh. Well, as long as it's not Riverdale. Those guys are still after me.

jokes.text: Hey, Mr. Smithers! Well, you might as well give me a ride home now.
jokes.setup: Anonymous informant trying to maintain secrecy
jokes.punchline: Bart immediately identifying Smithers, ruining the mysterious persona
jokes.explanation: Good deflation of mystery trope - anonymous informant immediately recognized. Smithers' resigned response is perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: I'm an anonymous informant. You can't know who I am.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Uh, Mr. Smithers?

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: Shh! I said you couldn't know who I am!

jokes.text: Sideshow Bob's ultra-conservative views... conflict with my choice of lifestyle.
jokes.setup: Smithers explaining why he's betraying Mr. Burns
jokes.punchline: Euphemistic reference to being gay in conservative politics
jokes.explanation: Subtle character development joke that acknowledges Smithers' sexuality and political conflict tactfully

dialogue.speaker: Smithers
dialogue.line: I'm sorry Mr. Burns, but I have to do this. You see, I've been living a lie all these years. I'm a... a... a Republican!

jokes.text: We're gonna have to move into a Motel 6. But Dad can't afford 6 dollars a night.
jokes.setup: Family facing homelessness
jokes.punchline: Bart's misunderstanding of motel pricing combined with Homer's poverty
jokes.explanation: Good child logic joke showing Bart's literal interpretation of brand name plus family financial reality

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: We can't afford a house anymore. We'll have to stay at a motel.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Cool! Do they have a magic fingers bed?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's not magic, boy. That's just a vibrating bed you have to pay for.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: How much?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: A quarter.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: A quarter?! We're rich!

jokes.text: So did Buddy Holly, Richie Valens, and the Big Bopper. Even the pet cemetery voted for Bob. Look. Mr. And Mrs. Bananas, Humphrey Boa-gart.
jokes.setup: Dead people voting illegally
jokes.punchline: Escalating from real dead celebrities to absurd pet names with puns
jokes.explanation: Good escalation from real to absurd with nice pet name puns. Humphrey Boa-gart is particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Election Official
dialogue.line: We've discovered dead people voting in this election.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: That's terrible! Who are they?

dialogue.speaker: Election Official
dialogue.line: Well, we've got Frank Sinatra, Marilyn Monroe, and John F. Kennedy.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: How is that even possible?

dialogue.speaker: Election Official
dialogue.line: It gets worse. We also found votes from Fluffy McWhiskers, Sir Barks-a-lot, and Tweety Von Feathers.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Those are... pet names.

dialogue.speaker: Election Official
dialogue.line: Exactly. And we're still finding more: Hammy Neutron, Goldie Hound, and a goldfish named Nemo-crat.

jokes.text: Oh, my poor dead kitty, please, not you too
jokes.setup: Finding pet cemetery votes
jokes.punchline: Lisa's personal emotional reaction to her dead pet being used in fraud
jokes.explanation: Sweet character moment showing Lisa's emotional connection even to election fraud involving her pet

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: My snowball... used in voter fraud? This is the worst day of my life.

jokes.text: All right, Bob, now it's personal. Hey. He did try to kill me.
jokes.setup: Lisa declaring the fight personal after seeing her dead cat voted
jokes.punchline: Bart's reminder that Bob already tried to murder him
jokes.explanation: Excellent character beat showing Lisa's priorities (pet over brother) and Bart's matter-of-fact delivery about attempted murder

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: This is personal now.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Lisa, he already tried to kill me.

jokes.text: That way, they can sleep soundly tonight on their hard, feculent motel pillows.
jokes.setup: Bob pretending to care about justice for children
jokes.punchline: Unnecessarily elaborate and cruel description using vocabulary word 'feculent'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Bob character moment - pretentious vocabulary used for petty cruelty. 'Feculent' is both accurate and unnecessarily fancy.

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: I care deeply about justice for children... those feculent little ankle-biters and their pestilent ways.

jokes.text: Birchibald T. Barlow!
jokes.setup: Lisa naming the supposed mastermind
jokes.punchline: Birch Barlow's ridiculously pretentious full name
jokes.explanation: Functional character name joke revealing Barlow's pompous full name

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: It was Birch Tarragon Olsen Barlow all along.

jokes.text: You were just Barlow's lackey. You were Ronnie to his Nancy. Sonny to his Cher. Ringo to his rest of the Beatles.
jokes.setup: Kids trying to provoke Bob by calling him a puppet
jokes.punchline: Escalating series of subordinate relationships, ending with Ringo insult
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalating insults with good structure. The Ringo reference is particularly harsh and funny.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Hey Bob, you're a puppet!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Yeah, and that makes you subordinate to the puppeteer.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Which makes the puppeteer subordinate to the guy pulling his strings.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Who's subordinate to the network executives.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Who answer to the sponsors.

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Who are all subordinate to Ringo Starr.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Ringo Starr?!

jokes.text: There. Is that what you want, you smarmy little bastards?
jokes.setup: Bob being goaded into confession
jokes.punchline: Bob's intellectual facade completely dropping with crude insult
jokes.explanation: Great character break - Bob's refined persona cracking under pressure with surprisingly crude language

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: You think you're so clever, don't you? Well, let me tell you something about your so-called 'superior intellect'...

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: All those years of Shakespeare, all those years of Krusty's seltzer bottles—they mean NOTHING! You're just a bald-headed moron in orange shorts!

jokes.text: You want the truth? You can't handle the truth. No truth-handler, you. I deride your truth-handling abilities.
jokes.setup: Famous 'A Few Good Men' quote
jokes.punchline: Bob's unnecessarily elaborate and redundant expansion of the movie line
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character moment - Bob can't help but over-elaborate even famous movie quotes. 'No truth-handler, you' is perfectly pretentious and awkward.

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: You can't handle the truth! You see, the truth is a complex philosophical construct that exists in multiple dimensions of reality, encompassing not only the factual accuracy of statements and assertions, but also the deeper metaphysical nature of existence itself, which cannot be easily quantified or simplified into basic binary concepts of true or false, right or wrong, guilty or innocent, and therefore, I posit to you that your capacity to comprehend, process, and ultimately handle such a multifaceted and elaborate concept is, in fact, severely limited by the constraints of your human cognition!

jokes.text: Because you need me, Springfield. Your guilty conscience may force you to vote Democratic. But deep down inside, you secretly long for a cold-hearted Republican... to lower taxes, brutalize criminals and rule you like a king.
jokes.setup: Bob explaining his political philosophy
jokes.punchline: Honest description of authoritarian appeal disguised as civic duty
jokes.explanation: Excellent political satire that captures authoritarian appeal through Bob's intellectual arrogance. Dark but insightful.

dialogue.speaker: Sideshow Bob
dialogue.line: You see, my political philosophy is really quite simple. I believe in order, discipline, and the removal of all those tedious democratic processes that slow down progress. Of course, I frame it as civic duty and the greater good.

jokes.text: Tomorrow we were gonna find out who the dish ran away with. The spoon, Bart. Of course.
jokes.setup: Ralph excited about kindergarten lesson
jokes.punchline: His naive anticipation of learning obvious nursery rhyme ending
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ralph moment showing his innocent excitement about obvious things paired with Lisa's gentle correction

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: I'm going to learn so much today! Miss Hoover said we're studying the alphabet song!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Ralph, you've known the alphabet song since kindergarten.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: But Lisa, I heard there's a SECRET ending nobody knows about!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Ralph... the song ends with Z.

dialogue.speaker: Ralph
dialogue.line: Now I know my ABCs, next time won't you sing with me?

jokes.text: Say, Terwilliger's a Yalie. Bob, come along. We need an eighth to row against the Princeton alums. Princeton?
jokes.setup: Bob lamenting his prison conditions
jokes.punchline: Prison revealed to be filled with Ivy League alumni having crew competitions
jokes.explanation: Brilliant subversion of prison expectations - white-collar criminals maintaining Ivy League rivalries in jail. Bob's disgusted 'Princeton?' is perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Bob
dialogue.line: I can't believe I'm stuck in this terrible prison with all these criminals.

dialogue.speaker: Prison Guard
dialogue.line: Actually, this is a minimum security facility for white-collar offenders. Most of our inmates are Ivy League alumni.

dialogue.speaker: Inmate 1
dialogue.line: Row, row, row your boat!

dialogue.speaker: Inmate 2
dialogue.line: Come on crew team, we've got the regatta in twenty minutes!

tmdb_overview: Mayor Quimby pardons attempted murderer Sideshow Bob, who then runs against Quimby for mayor.
insight: Political satire drives 52 jokes in 22 minutes—Sideshow Bob's mayoral arc peaks at 84.5.
